name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُۥٓ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٢٢)
```

```english
When he came of age, We gave him judgement and [sacred] knowledge, and thus do We reward the virtuous. (22)
```

```arabic
وَرَٰوَدَتْهُ ٱلَّتِى هُوَ فِى بَيْتِهَا عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ وَغَلَّقَتِ ٱلْأَبْوَٰبَ وَقَالَتْ هَيْتَ لَكَ ۚ قَالَ مَعَاذَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ رَبِّىٓ أَحْسَنَ مَثْوَاىَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٢٣)
```

```english
The woman in whose house he was, solicited him. She closed the doors and said, ‘Come!!’ He said, ‘God forbid! Indeed He is my Lord; He has given me a good abode. Indeed the wrongdoers are not felicitous.’ (23)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِۦ ۖ وَهَمَّ بِهَا لَوْلَآ أَن رَّءَا بُرْهَـٰنَ رَبِّهِۦ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ لِنَصْرِفَ عَنْهُ ٱلسُّوٓءَ وَٱلْفَحْشَآءَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (٢٤)
```

```english
She certainly made for him; and he would have made for her [too] had he not beheld the proof of his Lord. So it was, that We might turn away from him all evil and indecency. He was indeed one of Our dedicated servants. (24)
```

```arabic
وَٱسْتَبَقَا ٱلْبَابَ وَقَدَّتْ قَمِيصَهُۥ مِن دُبُرٍ وَأَلْفَيَا سَيِّدَهَا لَدَا ٱلْبَابِ ۚ قَالَتْ مَا جَزَآءُ مَنْ أَرَادَ بِأَهْلِكَ سُوٓءًا إِلَّآ أَن يُسْجَنَ أَوْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٢٥)
```

```english
They raced to the door, and she tore his shirt from behind, and they ran into her husband at the door. She said, ‘What is to be the requital of him who has evil intentions for your wife except imprisonment or a painful punishment?’ (25)
```

```arabic
قَالَ هِىَ رَٰوَدَتْنِى عَن نَّفْسِى ۚ وَشَهِدَ شَاهِدٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِهَآ إِن كَانَ قَمِيصُهُۥ قُدَّ مِن قُبُلٍ فَصَدَقَتْ وَهُوَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٢٦)
```

```english
He said, ‘It was she who solicited me.’ A witness of her own household testified [thus]: ‘If his shirt is torn from the front, she tells the truth and he lies. (26)
```

```arabic
وَإِن كَانَ قَمِيصُهُۥ قُدَّ مِن دُبُرٍ فَكَذَبَتْ وَهُوَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٢٧)
```

```english
But if his shirt is torn from behind, then she lies and he tells the truth.’ (27)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا رَءَا قَمِيصَهُۥ قُدَّ مِن دُبُرٍ قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ مِن كَيْدِكُنَّ ۖ إِنَّ كَيْدَكُنَّ عَظِيمٌ (٢٨)
```

```english
So when he saw that his shirt was torn from behind, he said, ‘This is [a case] of you women’s guile! Your guile is indeed great! (28)
```

```arabic
يُوسُفُ أَعْرِضْ عَنْ هَـٰذَا ۚ وَٱسْتَغْفِرِى لِذَنۢبِكِ ۖ إِنَّكِ كُنتِ مِنَ ٱلْخَاطِـِٔينَ (٢٩)
```

```english
Joseph, let this matter alone, and you, woman, plead for forgiveness for your sin, for you have indeed been erring.’ (29)
```

```arabic
۞ وَقَالَ نِسْوَةٌ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ ٱمْرَأَتُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ تُرَٰوِدُ فَتَىٰهَا عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ ۖ قَدْ شَغَفَهَا حُبًّا ۖ إِنَّا لَنَرَىٰهَا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٣٠)
```

```english
Some of the townswomen said, ‘The chieftain’s wife has solicited her slave boy! He has captivated her love. Indeed we see her to be in manifest error.’ (30)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا سَمِعَتْ بِمَكْرِهِنَّ أَرْسَلَتْ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَعْتَدَتْ لَهُنَّ مُتَّكَـًٔا وَءَاتَتْ كُلَّ وَٰحِدَةٍ مِّنْهُنَّ سِكِّينًا وَقَالَتِ ٱخْرُجْ عَلَيْهِنَّ ۖ فَلَمَّا رَأَيْنَهُۥٓ أَكْبَرْنَهُۥ وَقَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ وَقُلْنَ حَـٰشَ لِلَّهِ مَا هَـٰذَا بَشَرًا إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا مَلَكٌ كَرِيمٌ (٣١)
```

```english
When she heard of their machinations, she sent for them and arranged a repast, and gave each of them a knife, and said [to Joseph], ‘Come out before them.’ So when they saw him, they marvelled at him and cut their hands [absent-mindedly], and they said, ‘Good heavens! This is not a human being! This is but a noble angel!’ (31)
```

```arabic
قَالَتْ فَذَٰلِكُنَّ ٱلَّذِى لُمْتُنَّنِى فِيهِ ۖ وَلَقَدْ رَٰوَدتُّهُۥ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ فَٱسْتَعْصَمَ ۖ وَلَئِن لَّمْ يَفْعَلْ مَآ ءَامُرُهُۥ لَيُسْجَنَنَّ وَلَيَكُونًا مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰغِرِينَ (٣٢)
```

```english
She said, ‘He is the one on whose account you blamed me. Certainly, I did solicit him, but he was continent, and if he does not do what I bid him, surely he shall be imprisoned and humbled.’ (32)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱلسِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَىَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِىٓ إِلَيْهِ ۖ وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّى كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (٣٣)
```

```english
He said, ‘My Lord! The prison is dearer to me than to what they invite me. If You do not turn away their schemes from me, I will incline towards them and become one of the senseless.’ (33)
```

```arabic
فَٱسْتَجَابَ لَهُۥ رَبُّهُۥ فَصَرَفَ عَنْهُ كَيْدَهُنَّ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٣٤)
```

```english
So his Lord answered him and turned away their stratagems from him. Indeed, He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (34)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses encompass the story of Yusuf (peace be upon him) during the days of his stay in the house of the *Aziz* (the Mighty One of Egypt). In this period, he was tested by the love the wife of the *Aziz* harbored for him and her attempt to seduce him. He was further afflicted by the infatuation of the women of the city and their attempts to entice him. This was a profound trial (بلوى), through which an extraordinary degree of chastity (عفة) and purity of character (طهارة الذيل) became manifest, and even more wondrous was his total absorption (توله) in the love of his Lord.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*And when he reached his full maturity, We gave him judgment and knowledge, and thus do We reward the righteous*» (وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ آتَيْناهُ حُكْماً وَعِلْماً وَكَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ). Reaching full maturity (بلوغ الأشد) refers to a person reaching an age where the physical powers of the body become firm and the limbs strengthen as the traces of childhood vanish. This typically begins from the age of eighteen and continues until middle age, at which point the intellect (العقل) reaches perfection and maturity (الرشد) is complete.

It appears that what is intended here is reaching the beginning of youth, rather than its middle or its end (such as the age of forty). The evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted, regarding Musa (peace be upon him): «*And when he reached his full maturity and became firm, We gave him judgment and knowledge*» (وَلَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ وَاسْتَوى آتَيْناهُ حُكْماً وَعِلْماً) (Al-Qasas: 14), where He indicated the middle of that stage with the word "became firm" (اسْتَوى). Furthermore, His saying: «*until, when he reaches his full maturity and reaches forty years, he says, ‘My Lord! Inspire me to give thanks for Your blessing...’*» (حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ وَبَلَغَ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً قالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِي أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ) (Al-Ahqaf: 15). If reaching full maturity were synonymous with reaching forty, there would have been no need to repeat the word "reaches" (بَلَغَ).

Thus, there is no merit to what some have mentioned—that reaching full maturity means reaching thirty or thirty-three—nor to what others have said—that it means reaching forty. Moreover, it would be absurd to suggest that the wife of the *Aziz* waited throughout Yusuf’s prime and the bloom of his youth, only to become infatuated and attempt to seduce him when he reached forty and approached old age.

Regarding His saying: «*We gave him judgment*» (آتَيْناهُ حُكْماً), judgment (الحكم) is the decisive word (القول الفصل) and the removal of doubt and uncertainty from matters subject to disagreement—as derived from the linguistic root. Its necessary consequence is the correctness of vision in general human knowledge pertaining to the Origin (المبدأ), the Return (المعاد), spiritual ethics (الأخلاق النفسانية), and the laws and etiquettes (الشرائع والآداب) related to human society.

In light of his (peace be upon him) saying to his two companions in prison: «*Judgment belongs only to Allah*» (إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ) (Verse 40 of the Surah), and his subsequent saying: «*The matter about which you both inquire has been decided*» (قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ الَّذِي فِيهِ تَسْتَفْتِيانِ) (Verse 41), it is known that this judgment (الحكم) granted to him was the judgment of Allah; thus, his judgment was Allah’s judgment. This is precisely what Ibrahim (peace be upon him) asked of his Lord when he said: «*My Lord! Grant me judgment and join me with the righteous*» (رَبِّ هَبْ لِي حُكْماً وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ) (Ash-Shu'ara: 83).

Regarding His saying: «*and knowledge*» (وَعِلْماً), this mentioned knowledge, attributed to the granting of the Exalted, regardless of its nature or extent, is a knowledge (علم) unmixed with ignorance (جهل). Similarly, the judgment (الحكم) mentioned with it is a judgment unmixed with personal whim (هوى نفساني) or satanic suggestion (تسويل شيطاني). How could it be otherwise? For the One who granted them both is Allah, the Glorified, and He has said: «*And Allah is dominant over His affairs*» (وَاللهُ غالِبٌ عَلى أَمْرِهِ) (Verse 21), and: «*Indeed Allah carries through His command*» (إِنَّ اللهَ بالِغُ أَمْرِهِ) (At-Talaq: 3). Therefore, the judgment He grants is unmixed with the instability of doubt and uncertainty, and the knowledge He grants can never be ignorance.

Furthermore, it is understood that these divine gifts are not haphazard acts, nor are they futile or vain on the part of the Exalted. The souls granted this judgment and knowledge are not equal to the erring souls who are submerged in their ignorance. Allah the Exalted has said: «*As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the leave of its Lord, and as for that which is bad, it does not yield except scantily*» (وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَباتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ وَالَّذِي خَبُثَ لا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِداً) (Al-A'raf: 58). This is alluded to by His saying: «*and thus do We reward the righteous*» (وَكَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ), which indicates that this judgment and knowledge were not primary gifts without any prerequisite; rather, they were a form of reward (جزاء) with which Allah recompensed him for being among the righteous (المحسنين).

It is not far-fetched to infer from His saying: «*and thus do We reward the righteous*» that Allah the Exalted rewards every righteous person—according to their varying degrees of righteousness—with a portion of judgment and knowledge suitable to their station in righteousness. Allah the Exalted has said: «*O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and believe in His Apostle. He will grant you a double share of His mercy and give you a light to walk by*» (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَآمِنُوا بِرَسُولِهِ يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ نُوراً تَمْشُونَ بِهِ) (Al-Hadid: 28), and: «*Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people...*» (أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأحيَيناهُ وَجَعَلنا لَهُ نوراً يَمشي بِهِ فِي النّاسِ) (Al-An'am: 122).

This knowledge mentioned in the verse includes what Allah promised to teach Yusuf regarding the interpretation of dreams (تأويل الأحاديث). This is because it falls between His saying in the preceding verses: «*and that We might teach him the interpretation of dreams*» (وَلِنُعَلِّمَهُ مِنْ تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحادِيثِ) and His saying, narrating Yusuf’s words to his prison companions: «*That is of what my Lord has taught me*» (ذلِكُما مِمَّا عَلَّمَنِي رَبِّي). Understand this well.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*And she in whose house he was, solicited him, and she shut the doors and said, ‘Come!’ He said, ‘Allah forbid! Indeed he is my lord; he has given me a goodly lodging. Indeed the wrongdoers do not prosper’*» (وَراوَدَتْهُ الَّتِي هُوَ فِي بَيْتِها عَنْ نَفْسِهِ وَغَلَّقَتِ الْأَبْوابَ وَقالَتْ هَيْتَ لَكَ قالَ مَعاذَ اللهِ إِنَّهُ رَبِّي أَحْسَنَ مَثْوايَ إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: **Al-Rawd** (الرود) is to go to and fro in seeking something gently; from it comes the term *ra'id* (رائد) for one seeking pasture. He said: *al-iradah* (الإرادة - will/desire) is derived from *rada yarudu* (راد يرود) when one strives to seek something. He said: **al-murawadah** (المراودة - solicitation/enticement) is to contend with another regarding their will, desiring something other than what they desire, or seeking something other than what they seek. "I solicited so-and-so regarding such-and-such." The Exalted said: «*It was she who solicited me*» (هِيَ راوَدَتْنِي عَنْ نَفْسِي) and: «*soliciting her slave-boy*» (تُراوِدُ فَتاها عَنْ نَفْسِهِ), meaning she tried to turn him away from his own intent. Similarly: «*I did solicit him*» (وَلَقَدْ راوَدْتُهُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ) and «*We will solicit his father for him*» (سَنُراوِدُ عَنْهُ أَباهُ). [End of quote].

In *al-Majma'*: *al-murawadah* is to request a matter with gentleness and softness so that it may be acted upon. From it comes the term *mirwad* (مرود - a probe/applicator) because it is used gently. One does not say *rawadahu* when demanding a debt. Its origin is from *rada yarudu* when seeking pasture. In the proverb: "The scout (الرائد) does not lie to his people." *Al-taghliq* (التغليق) is to shut the door in a way that makes it difficult to open. The intensive form (شدد) is used to indicate the closing of many doors or to emphasize the security of the locking. [End of quote].

*Hayta laka* (هَيْتَ لَكَ) is a verbal noun meaning "Come!" or "I am yours." *Ma'adh Allah* (مَعاذَ اللهِ) means "I seek refuge in Allah (معاذا)"; it is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) standing in place of its verb.

The noble verse «*And she in whose house he was, solicited him, and she shut the doors and said, ‘Come!’ He said, ‘Allah forbid! Indeed he is my lord; he has given me a goodly lodging. Indeed the wrongdoers do not prosper’*», despite its brevity, informs us of the general story of the solicitation. However, contemplation (التدبر) of the constraints employed, the context in which it occurs, and other aspects of his story mentioned in the Surah, clarifies the reality of the situation and removes the veil from the details of what was hidden.

## Yusuf:

Here is a young child whom the hands of destiny have moved to the house of the *Aziz*, bearing the mark of slaves. Perhaps he was asked nothing but his name, and spoke nothing but "My name is Yusuf," or that was said of him. Nothing was apparent from his dialect except that he had been raised among the Hebrews. He was not asked about his home or lineage, for slaves have no homes, and it was not customary to preserve the lineages of bondsmen. He remained silent, his tongue sealed, speaking of nothing, though many a story lay within his breast. His lineage was not known until years later when he said to his prison companions: «*I follow the creed of my fathers, Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya‘qub*» (وَاتَّبَعْتُ مِلَّةَ آبائِي إِبْراهِيمَ وَإِسْحاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ). Nor did he disclose the monotheism (التوحيد) of his servitude to Allah among those idolaters except what he mentioned to the wife of the *Aziz* when she solicited him, saying: «*Allah forbid! Indeed He is my Lord...*»

Today, he is an ally of silence, yet his heart is full of what he witnesses of Allah’s subtle handiwork with him. He remembers what his father, the Prophet Ya'qub, instilled in him regarding the reality of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and the meaning of servitude (العبودية). He remembers the glad tidings of the vision—that Allah would choose him for Himself and join him with his fathers Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya'qub. He does not forget what his brothers did to him, nor what his Lord promised him in the depths of the well when he was cut off from all means: that he was under Divine Guardianship (الولاية الإلهية) and Lordly Upbringing (التربية الربوبية), cared for in his affairs, and that he would surely inform his brothers of this deed of theirs while they were unaware.

Thus, he (peace be upon him) was full of perception, his soul immersed in witnessing the hidden graces of his Lord. He saw himself under the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah, delighted by His beautiful handiwork, encountering nothing but good and facing nothing but beauty.

This is what eased the calamities that befell him and the successive trials and tribulations. He bore them despite their bitterness, neither complaining, nor despairing, nor losing his way. He mentioned this to his brothers when he identified himself to them, saying: «*Indeed whoever is God-conscious and patient—Allah does not let the reward of the righteous go to waste*» (إِنَّهُ مَنْ يَتَّقِ وَيَصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ) (Verse 90).

Yusuf’s soul continued to be drawn to the beautiful handiwork of his Lord, his heart deeply contemplating the subtle signs pointing toward Him. Every day, his love increased through the evidence of guardianship (الولاية) he found, witnessing that his Lord is the One who maintains every soul according to what it has earned, and that He is a witness over all things. Eventually, Divine Love (المحبة الإلهية) took hold of him, and total absorption and enamorment (الوله والهيمان) settled in his innermost being. His sole concern was his Lord; no distraction could occupy him, nor could any diverter turn him away, even for the blink of an eye. This is remarkably clear to anyone who contemplates the dialogues recounted in the Surah, such as his saying: «*Allah forbid! Indeed He is my Lord*», and: «*It is not for us to ascribe any partner to Allah*», and: «*Judgment belongs only to Allah*», and: «*You are my guardian in this world and the Hereafter*», and others, as we shall explain, Allah willing.

This was the state of Yusuf (peace be upon him). He was a form behind which lay nothing but Divine Love, which made him forget himself and occupied him from all else—an image whose meaning was that he was pure (خالص), whom Allah had purified (أخلص) for Himself, such that no one shared him with Him.

To the *Aziz*, from the first day he entered his house, Yusuf appeared as nothing more than a small Hebrew boy, his owned slave. However, the *Aziz’s* words to his wife: «*Make his stay honorable. Maybe he will be of use to us, or we may adopt him as a son*» (أَكْرِمِي مَثْواهُ عَسى أَنْ يَنْفَعَنا أَوْ نَتَّخِذَهُ وَلَداً) reveal that he saw in him a dignity and composure, discerning a greatness and psychological majesty that made him hope to benefit from him or join him to himself through adoption, in addition to Yusuf’s wondrous beauty and grace.

## The Wife of the Aziz:

The wife of the *Aziz*—and she was the noble lady of Egypt—was commanded by the *Aziz* to make Yusuf’s stay honorable. He informed her that he had a purpose and a hope for him. She did not cease to strive in honoring Yusuf, making his stay pleasant and attending to his affairs—not as one attends to a common slave, but as one cares for a precious gem or a piece of one's own heart. She loved him for his singular beauty and abundant perfection. As the days passed, her love grew upon love until he reached puberty and attained the stature of manhood. She could no longer restrain herself from falling passionately in love with him, humbling herself—despite her royal status, might, and the protection of her chastity and seclusion—before the passion residing in her secret heart, which had taken hold of her entire being.

Yusuf was constantly in her company, never leaving her side. She was a noblewoman whose command was not refused and whose resolve was not turned back. She considered herself Yusuf’s mistress and him her owned slave, who had no choice but to obey and submit to her. The houses of kings and the powerful have the means to achieve their various goals and desires through all sorts of ruses and schemes; for them, most means, however difficult for others, are made easy. Furthermore, she possessed beauty and adornment, for the harems of kings are not entered by the unsightly, but only by beautiful, captivating women.

Custom dictates that these factors—having converged upon the noble lady of Egypt—kindled every flame in her heart and stoked every fire, until she was submerged in her love for Yusuf, distracted by her passion for him from everything else. He surrounded her heart from every side; he was the first of her speech when she spoke, and in her mind when she was silent. She had no concern but Yusuf, and no desire but for him: «*He has smitten her to the heart with love*» (قَدْ شَغَفَها حُبًّا). And Yusuf possessed the beauty that captivates hearts—how much more so when he is the constant focus of an enamored, infatuated lover?

## Yusuf and the Wife of the Aziz:

The noble lady of Egypt continued to prepare herself and hope for union with Yusuf and the attainment of what she desired from him. She was affectionate in her dealings with him, accompanying this with the coquetry and allure (الغنج والدلال) characteristic of beauties, seeking to trap him with what she possessed, just as he had trapped her with what he possessed. Perhaps the patience and silence she witnessed from Yusuf emboldened her in what she aimed for and enticed her toward him.

When her soul yearned for him intensely and her passion reached its peak, and all other paths failed her, she secluded herself with him in her house, having shut the doors so that none remained but she and Yusuf. She did not doubt that Yusuf would obey her command, as she had always seen him characterized by hearing and obeying, and she perceived that the current circumstances and conditions dictated her success in obtaining what she wanted from him.

A youth absorbed in his love [for God] and a woman yearning in her passion met in a secluded house. As for her, she was infatuated with the love of Yusuf, wanting to turn him away from himself toward her, employing the shutting of doors, solicitation, and relying on her might and status, calling him to herself with the language of command: «*Come!*» (هَيْتَ لَكَ) to compel him to do what she wanted.

As for him, he was submerged in the love of his Lord, having purified and clarified his soul for Him. He left no place in his heart for anything other than his Beloved. He was in seclusion with his Lord, in His presence, witnessing His beauty and majesty. Cosmic causes (الأسباب الكونية), despite their apparent influence, had vanished from his sight. Contrary to them, he did not exult in worldly means nor lean upon external supports.

She saw herself overcoming him through means—shutting the doors, solicitation, and the command: «*Come!*» As for him, he met her with his saying: «*Allah forbid!*» (مَعاذَ اللهِ). He did not answer her with a threat, nor did he say, "I fear the *Aziz*," or "I will not betray him," or "I am from the house of prophecy and purity," or "My chastity or my protection (عصمتي) prevents me from indecency," nor did he say, "I hope for Allah’s reward or fear His punishment," and so on. Had his heart been attached to any of the apparent means, he would have mentioned it and begun with it upon the sudden onset of distress and the descent of necessity, as is the requirement of human nature.

Rather, he held fast to the firm handle of monotheism (التوحيد) and answered only by seeking refuge in Allah. There was no one in his heart besides his Lord, nor did his sight cross Him to another. This is the pure monotheism (التوحيد الخالص) to which Divine Love had guided him and in which he was absorbed in his Lord, making him forget all means, even making him forget himself. He did not say, "I seek refuge from you in Allah," or anything to that effect; he simply said: «*Allah forbid!*» (مَعاذَ اللهِ). How great is the difference between this saying of his and the saying of Maryam to the Spirit when he appeared to her as a well-proportioned man: «*I seek the protection of the All-beneficent from you, should you be God-conscious*» (إِنِّي أَعُوذُ بِالرَّحْمنِ مِنْكَ إِنْ كُنْتَ تَقِيًّا) (Maryam: 18).

As for his second statement to her: «*Indeed he is my lord; he has given me a goodly lodging. Indeed the wrongdoers do not prosper*» (إِنَّهُ رَبِّي أَحْسَنَ مَثْوايَ إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ), it clarifies and illuminates the word of monotheism (التوحيد) he expressed by saying: «*Allah forbid!*». He says: "What I witness is that your honoring of my stay—resulting from the *Aziz’s* word to you: ‘Make his stay honorable’—is an act of my Lord and a kindness from Him to me. Thus, my Lord has given me a goodly lodging, and even if it is attributed to you in some way, He is the One in Whom I must seek refuge and to Whom I must turn. I seek refuge in Him because answering what you ask and committing this sin is wrongdoing (ظلم), and the wrongdoers do not prosper; thus, there is no way to commit it."

Through his saying: «*Indeed He is my Lord; He has given me a goodly lodging*», he conveyed several points:

First: That he is a monotheist (موحد) who does not recognize the polytheism (شرك) of idolatry. He is not among those who take lords besides Allah, as the idolaters do, taking other lords with Allah to whom they attribute the governance of the world. Rather, he says that Allah is his Lord, and there is no lord besides Him.

Second: That he is not among those who profess the oneness of Allah (التوحيد) in speech but associate partners with Him in action by granting independence to these apparent means, as if they affect what they affect by Allah’s permission. Rather, he sees the beautiful effects attributed to means as the beautiful act of Allah the Exalted within that very attribution. While the wife of the *Aziz* sees that she is the one who honored his stay based on the *Aziz’s* instruction, and that she and her husband are his lords who manage his affairs, he sees that Allah the Glorified is the One who gave him a goodly lodging, and that He is his Lord who manages his affairs; therefore, he must seek refuge in Him.

Third: That he only sought refuge in Allah from what she called him to because it is wrongdoing (ظلم), and the one tainted by it does not prosper, nor is he guided to his happiness, nor can he be established in the presence of security with his Lord, as Allah the Exalted said, narrating the words of his grandfather Ibrahim (peace be upon him): «*Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be security, and they are the [rightly] guided*» (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ) (Al-An'am: 82).

Fourth: That he is a servant (مربوب)—meaning owned and governed—by Allah the Glorified, having no authority over anything, and possessing for himself neither benefit nor harm except what Allah wills for him or loves for him to do. Therefore, he did not reject what she asked of him with explicit language, but rather through allusion (الكناية), by saying: «*Allah forbid!*» etc. He did not say, "I will not do what you command me," nor "I will not commit such-and-such," nor "I seek refuge in Allah from you," or similar phrases, out of caution against claiming power or strength for himself, and out of fear of the mark of polytheism (الشرك) and ignorance (الجهالة). The only exception is in his saying: «*Indeed He is my Lord; He has given me a goodly lodging*», where he referred to himself twice, but only to affirm his state of being a servant (المربوبية) and to emphasize his humility and need. For this very reason, he replaced "honoring" (الإكرام) with "goodly treatment" (الإحسان), using «*He has given me a goodly lodging*» (أَحْسَنَ مَثْوايَ) in response to the *Aziz’s* «*Make his stay honorable*» (أَكْرِمِي مَثْواهُ), because "honoring" implies a respect for and exaltation of the persona.

In summary, although the event was a confrontation and a struggle between the wife of the *Aziz* and Yusuf (peace be upon him) according to the outward appearance, it was in reality a conflict between Divine Love and absorption (هيمان إلهي) and animalistic passion and lust (عشق وغرام حيواني), both contending for Yusuf, each pulling him toward itself. And the word of Allah was the highest; the heavenly, divine attraction took him, and Divine Love defended him, and Allah is dominant over His affair.

His saying, the Exalted: «*And she in whose house he was, solicited him*» (وَراوَدَتْهُ الَّتِي هُوَ فِي بَيْتِها عَنْ نَفْسِهِ) indicates the origin of the solicitation. The use of the description "she in whose house he was" indicates that the circumstances and conditions were in her favor and that the matter was difficult for him. Similarly, His saying: «*and she shut the doors*» (وَغَلَّقَتِ الْأَبْوابَ), where He used the intensive form *taghliq*, indicates emphasis, and He applied the shutting to "the doors" (الأبواب), which is a plural noun with the definite article. Likewise, His saying: «*and said, ‘Come!’*» (وَقالَتْ هَيْتَ لَكَ), where He used a masterly command indicating the exercise of authority and lordship, while suggesting that she had prepared for him from herself such that there was nothing between him and her desire except Yusuf’s mere acceptance. Nor was there anything between Yusuf—given the causes and conditions she had prepared and organized according to her claim—and accepting her, except that Allah was closer to Yusuf than his own self and closer than the *Aziz’s* wife, and to Allah the Glorified belongs all might.

His saying: «*He said, ‘Allah forbid! Indeed He is my Lord; He has given me a goodly lodging’*» to the end of the verse, is Yusuf’s response, meeting her request by seeking refuge in Allah. He says: "I seek refuge in Allah (معاذا) from what you call me to, for He is my Lord who has taken charge of my affair and given me a goodly lodging, making me thereby happy and successful. If I were to commit this wrongdoing, I would be distanced from success and would exit from under His guardianship."

In this speech, he (peace be upon him) observed the entirety of the etiquette of servitude (أدب العبودية), as previously mentioned. He first used the "Majestic Name" (لفظة الجلالة) [Allah], then the attribute of Lordship (الربوبية), to indicate that he worships no lord other than Allah, the creed of his fathers Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya'qub.

A number of commentators have considered the possibility that the pronoun in His saying: «*Indeed he is my lord; he has given me a goodly lodging*» refers to the situation (الشأن), and the meaning is that "my lord and master, the *Aziz*—based on outward appearance, as he bought Yusuf from the travelers—has given me a goodly lodging by commanding you to honor my stay. If I were to answer you in what you ask, it would be a betrayal of him, and I would not betray him."

A similar view is held by some who say the pronoun refers to the *Aziz*, being the subject of *inna*, and its predicate is «*my lord*» (رَبِّي), while «*he has given me a goodly lodging*» (أَحْسَنَ مَثْوايَ) is a second predicate.

The flaw in this is that if it were so, it would have been more appropriate to say: "Indeed the betrayers do not prosper," as he said to the messenger while in prison: «*This, so that he may know that I did not betray him in [his] absence, and that Allah does not guide the knavery of the betrayers*» (ذلِكُما لِيَعْلَمَ أَنِّي لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ وَأَنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي كَيْدَ الْخائِنِينَ) (Verse 52); he did not say, "I did not wrong him in his absence."

Furthermore, he (peace be upon him) would never have considered the *Aziz* a lord (رب) for himself, for he was free and not his owned slave, even if people presumed so based on appearances. He said to one of his prison companions: «*Mention me to your lord*» (اذْكُرْنِي عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ) (Verse 42), and he said to the king’s messenger: «*Go back to your lord*» (ارْجِعْ إِلى رَبِّكَ) (Verse 51), yet he did not refer to the king as "my lord" according to their custom in mentioning kings. He also said to the king’s messenger: «*ask him, ‘What was the state of the women who cut their hands?’ Indeed my Lord knows their knavery*» (فَسْئَلْهُ ما بالُ النِّسْوَةِ اللَّاتِي قَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ إِنَّ رَبِّي بِكَيْدِهِنَّ عَلِيمٌ), where he takes Allah the Glorified as a Lord for himself in contrast to the messenger taking the king as a lord.

What we have mentioned is also supported by His saying in the following verse: «*had he not seen the proof of his Lord*» (لَوْ لا أَنْ رَأى بُرْهانَ رَبِّهِ).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*She certainly made for him; and he would have made for her [too] had he not seen the proof of his Lord. So it was, that We might turn away from him evil and indecency. Indeed he was one of Our devoted servants*» (وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ وَهَمَّ بِها لَوْ لا أَنْ رَأى بُرْهانَ رَبِّهِ كَذلِكَ لِنَصْرِفَ عَنْهُ السُّوءَ وَالْفَحْشاءَ إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبادِنَا الْمُخْلَصِينَ). Profound contemplation of all aspects of the story and careful consideration of the surrounding circumstances, causes, and conditions acting within it, reveal that Yusuf’s escape from it was nothing short of a miracle (خارق للعادة), an event more akin to a vision than to wakefulness.

Yusuf (peace be upon him) was a man, and it is the nature of men to be inclined toward women. He was a youth who had reached his full maturity, the time when desire boils and lust surges. He possessed a singular beauty that dazzles the mind and captivates the heart, and beauty and grace invite passion and joy. He was submerged in luxury and a pleasant life, delighted by an honorable lodging, which are among the strongest causes of desire and indulgence. The queen was a woman of exquisite beauty, as is the case with the consorts of kings and the great.

She was undoubtedly adorned with that which takes hold of every heart. She was the noble lady of Egypt, a passionate, infatuated lover for whom souls yearn, and she herself yearned for him. She had a history of honoring, kindness, and favor toward Yusuf, all of which "cuts the tongue" and silences a person. She approached him and called him to herself, and patience in the face of such advances is most difficult. This captivating woman solicited him and employed all the coquetry and allure (الغنج والدلال) in her power. She was persistent with him, even pulling him toward her until she tore his shirt, and patience with such persistence is even more difficult and arduous. She was a noblewoman whose command was not refused nor her opinion turned back. She was his mistress, to whom the *Aziz* had specifically entrusted him. They were in a magnificent palace of the kings, with splendid views that dazzle the eyes and invite every pleasant life.

They were in seclusion, the doors shut and the curtains drawn. He was not safe from harm if he refused. He was secure from the matter being exposed or the veil being lifted, for she was a noblewoman who held the means of concealment and obfuscation. This encounter was not a one-time opportunity, but a key to a long, pleasant life. Yusuf could have made this intimacy and passion a means to achieve many of life’s hopes and desires, such as power, status, and wealth.

These are formidable causes and factors that, had they been directed at a mountain, would have leveled it, or if they had approached a solid rock, would have melted it. There was nothing there that could be imagined as a deterrent except the fear of exposure, the nobility of Yusuf’s lineage, or the ugliness of betraying the *Aziz*.

As for the fear of exposure, it has already been mentioned that he was secure from it. Had anything become apparent, the noble lady could have interpreted it away, as she did when the matter of her solicitation became known; she schemed until she satisfied the *Aziz* such that he did not punish her for anything, and she turned the punishment upon Yusuf until he was imprisoned.

As for the nobility of lineage, if it were a deterrent, it would have deterred Yusuf’s brothers from that which is greater than adultery and more grievous in sin. They were the sons of Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya'qub, like Yusuf, yet it did not deter them. The nobility of lineage did not prevent them from intending to kill him, casting him into the depths of the well, selling him to travelers as a slave, and grieving their father, the Prophet Ya'qub (peace be upon him), until he wept so much that his eyes turned white.

As for the ugliness and prohibition of betrayal, it is one of the social laws (القوانين الاجتماعية). Social laws only exert their influence through the consequences they entail in the event of violation. This is only effective when a person is under the authority of an executive power and a just government. However, if the executive power is negligent or corrupt and thus ignores the crime, or if the crime is hidden from its sight or outside its jurisdiction, then these laws have no influence, as we shall discuss shortly.

Thus, Yusuf (peace be upon him) had nothing with which to defend himself and overcome these powerful causes acting against him except the principle of monotheism (أصل التوحيد), which is faith in Allah. Or, if you will, Divine Love (المحبة الإلهية) which filled his existence and occupied his heart, leaving no room for anything else, not even the space of a finger. This is what contemplation of the story yields. Let us return to the text of the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: «*She certainly made for him; and he would have made for her [too] had he not seen the proof of his Lord. So it was, that We might turn away from him evil and indecency. Indeed he was one of Our devoted servants*». There is no doubt that the verse points to the manner of Yusuf’s salvation from this peril. The context indicates that "turning away from him evil and indecency" means saving him from what was desired of him and requested through solicitation and seclusion. The referent of "So it was" (كَذلِكَ) is what is contained in His saying: «*had he not seen the proof of his Lord*».

Thus, the meaning of His saying: «*So it was, that We might turn away...*» to the end of the verse, is that since he (peace be upon him) was among Our devoted servants (المُخْلَصِينَ), We turned away from him evil and indecency through what he saw of the proof of his Lord. Therefore, seeing the proof of his Lord was the cause by which Allah the Glorified turned away evil and indecency from Yusuf (peace be upon him).

The necessary consequence of this is that the implied recompense for His saying: «*had he not seen the proof of his Lord*» is the commission of evil and indecency. This further implies that «*had he not seen*» etc., is a condition for His saying: «*and he would have made for her*» (وَهَمَّ بِها). This requires that the meaning of his "making for her" (همه بها), similar to her "making for him" (همها به), be the intent toward sin. In this case, his "making for her" falls under the condition. The meaning is that had he not seen the proof of his Lord, he would have made for her and would have been on the verge of committing [the sin]. For although *lawla* (لَوْ لا) is attached to the conditional tools, and grammarians have prohibited its recompense from preceding it by analogy with the conditional *in* (إن), His saying: «*and he would have made for her*» is not its recompense. Rather, it is an object of an oath by conjunction with His saying: «*She certainly made for him*» (وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ), and it carries the meaning of the recompense, sufficing for its mention. It is like saying: "By Allah, I will surely strike him if he strikes me," meaning: "By Allah, if he strikes me, I will strike him."

The meaning of the verse is: "By Allah, she certainly made for him, and by Allah, had he not seen the proof of his Lord, he would have made for her and would have been on the verge of falling into sin." We say "on the verge of falling" and not "he fell" because *al-hamm* (الهم - making for/intending), as has been said, is only used for that which is accompanied by a deterrent, as in His saying: «*and they meditated (هَمُّوا) what they could not attain*» (At-Tawbah: 74), and His saying: «*When two groups of you were about to (هَمَّتْ) lose heart*» (Al-Imran: 122), and the saying of Sakhr:

*I intend (أهم) a matter of resolve but cannot do it,*
*For the wild ass has been prevented from leaping.*

Had it not been for what he saw of the proof, what would have occurred was the intent and the approach, without the commission or the perpetration. Allah the Glorified pointed to this by saying: «*that We might turn away from him evil and indecency*»; He did not say: "that We might turn him away from evil and indecency." Contemplate this.

From here it becomes clear that it is more appropriate for "evil" (السوء) to refer to the intent toward her and the inclination to her, just as "indecency" (الفحشاء) refers to the perpetration of the lewd act, which is adultery. Thus, he (peace be upon him) neither did it nor was he on the verge of it; had it not been for what Allah showed him of the proof, he would have intended it and been on the verge of doing it. This meaning is what is supported by the rational consideration and reflection we presented regarding the causes and factors gathered at that moment, which were acting against him.

His saying, the Exalted: «*She certainly made for him*» (وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ), the *lam* here is for an oath. The meaning is: "I swear, she certainly intended Yusuf for what she wanted from him." Intent (الهم) only occurs when the will is accompanied by some action.

His saying: «*and he would have made for her had he not seen the proof of his Lord*» is conjoined to the object of the oath in the previous sentence. The meaning is: "I swear, had it not been for his seeing the proof of his Lord, he would have made for her and would have been on the verge of answering her in what she wanted from him."

The proof (البران) is the authority (السلطان). It refers to the cause that yields certainty due to its dominance over hearts, such as a miracle (المعجزة). Allah the Exalted said: «*These two shall be two proofs from your Lord to Pharaoh and his elite*» (Al-Qasas: 32), and: «*O mankind! Certainly a proof has come to you from your Lord*» (An-Nisa: 174), and: «*Is there any god besides Allah? Say, ‘Produce your proof, if you are truthful’*» (An-Naml: 64). It is the certain argument (الحجة اليقينية) that clarifies the truth and leaves no doubt for the doubter.

That which Yusuf (peace be upon him) saw of the proof of his Lord, although His speech does not clarify it entirely, was—in any case—one of the causes of certainty that is never joined by ignorance or misguidance. That it was of the nature of knowledge is indicated by Yusuf’s (peace be upon him) saying while supplicating his Lord, as will come: «*unless You turn away their knavery from me, I will yield to them and become one of the ignorant*» (وَإِلَّا تَصْرِفْ عَنِّي كَيْدَهُنَّ أَصْبُ إِلَيْهِنَّ وَأَكُنْ مِنَ الْجاهِلِينَ) (Verse 33). It also indicates that it is not the conventional knowledge (العلم المتعارف) regarding the beauty or ugliness of acts, or their benefit or harm, for this type of knowledge may be joined by misguidance and sin, as is apparent. Allah the Exalted said: «*Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god and whom Allah has led astray knowingly...*» (أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ وَأَضَلَّهُ اللهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ) (Al-Jathiyah: 23), and: «*They impugned them—though they were convinced in their hearts...*» (وَجَحَدُوا بِها وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْها أَنْفُسُهُمْ) (An-Naml: 14).

Thus, the proof He showed him—which is what Allah shows His devoted servants (عباده المخلصين)—is a type of unveiled knowledge (العلم المكشوف) and witnessed certainty (اليقين المشهود) which the human soul obeys with an obedience that never inclines toward sin at all. We shall provide some discussion on this, Allah willing.

His saying: «*So it was, that We might turn away from him evil and indecency*» (كَذلِكَ لِنَصْرِفَ عَنْهُ السُّوءَ وَالْفَحْشاءَ). The *lam* in "that We might turn away" (لِنَصْرِفَ) is for purpose or causation, and the outcome is the same. "So it was" (كَذلِكَ) relates to "that We might turn away," and the reference is to the mentioned seeing of the proof of his Lord. Evil (السوء) is that whose issuance from a servant, qua servant, is bad; it is absolute sin or the intent toward it. **Indecency** (الفحشاء) is the commission of heinous acts like adultery. It has already been mentioned that the apparent context applies "evil and indecency" to adultery and the intent toward it.

The meaning is: The purpose—or the cause—in his seeing the proof of his Lord was that We might turn away from him indecency and the intent toward it.

Among the subtle indications in the verse is His saying: «*that We might turn away from him evil and indecency*», where He made evil and indecency the objects turned away from him, rather than making him the object turned away from them. This is because the latter would suggest that there was something within him necessitating their commission, requiring him to be turned away from it. This would contradict His testimony that he was among His devoted servants (عباده المخلصين), who are those whom Allah has purified for Himself, such that nothing shares them with Him. They do not obey anything other than Him—neither the suggestion of a devil, nor the adornment of the self, nor any caller that calls to other than Allah the Glorified.

His saying: «*Indeed he was one of Our devoted servants*» (إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبادِنَا الْمُخْلَصِينَ) is in the position of providing the reason for His saying: «*So it was, that We might turn away...*» etc. The meaning is: We treated Yusuf in this manner because he was among Our devoted servants, and they are treated in this way.

It appears from the verse that it is the characteristic of the devoted servants of Allah to see the proof of their Lord, and that Allah the Glorified turns away every evil and indecency from them. Thus, they neither commit sin nor intend it, because of the proof Allah shows them. This is Divine Protection (العصمة الإلهية) — *the state of being divinely shielded from sin and error*.

It also appears that this proof is a certain, cognitive cause (سبب علمي يقيني), but not of the conventional sciences known to us.

The commentators among the general and specific [schools] have various opinions regarding the interpretation of the verse:

**1 - Among them:** What some have mentioned and attributed to Ibn Abbas, Mujahid, Qatadah, Ikrimah, al-Hasan, and others: that the meaning is that she intended the lewd act, and he intended the same, and had he not seen the proof of his Lord, he would have done it.

They have described his "intent" (هم) in a manner from which the station of Prophecy is far removed and the sanctuary of the Truthful (الصديق) is purified. They mentioned that he intended the lewd act with her and drew so close to her that he loosened his waistband and sat in the position of a man with a woman, until the proof from his Lord reached him, nullifying the desire and saving him from destruction. They mentioned many different things in describing this proof.

Al-Ghazali said in his commentary on this Surah: They differed regarding it—meaning the proof—what was it? Some said: A bird landed on his shoulder and said in his ear: "Do not do it, for if you do, you will fall from the rank of the Prophets." It was said: He saw Ya'qub biting his finger, saying: "O Yusuf, do you not see me?" Al-Hasan al-Basri said: He saw her covering something and asked her: "What are you doing?" She said: "I am covering the face of my idol so it does not see me." Yusuf said: "You are ashamed before an inanimate object that neither understands nor sees; I am more worthy of being ashamed before the One who sees me and knows my secret and my public [affairs]."

The masters of language said: It was called out in his secret heart: "O Yusuf, your name is written in the register of the Prophets, and you wish to do the act of the foolish?" It was said: He saw a palm emerge from the wall on which was written: «*And do not approach fornication. Indeed it is an indecency and an evil way*». It was said: The ceiling of the house split open and he saw a beautiful form saying: "O Messenger of Protection, do not do it, for you are protected." It was said: He lowered his head and saw written on the ground: «*Whoever does evil shall be requited for it*». It was said: An angel came to him and wiped his wings over his back, and his desire exited from his toes. It was said: He saw the King in the house saying: "Am I not here?" It was said: A veil fell between them such that neither could see the other. It was said: He saw a maiden from the maidens of Paradise and was dazzled by her beauty; he said to her: "To whom do you belong?" She said: "To the one who does not commit adultery."

It was said: A bird passed over him and called out: "O Yusuf, do not hasten, for she is lawful for you and was created for you." It was said: He saw that well which was before him, with an angel standing over it saying: "O Yusuf, have you forgotten this well?" It was said: He saw Zulaykha in an ugly form and fled from her. It was said: He saw a person who said: "O Yusuf, look to your right." He looked and saw a serpent greater than any could be, which said: "The adulterer shall be in my belly tomorrow," so he fled from it. [End of quote].

Among what was said regarding it is that Ya'qub was personified for him and struck him in his chest, a blow by which his desire exited from his fingertips; this was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Mujahid, Ikrimah, and Ibn Jubayr, among other various views reported in the traditional commentaries.

The response to this—in addition to the fact that he (peace be upon him) was a Prophet possessing Divine Protection (عصمة إلهية) that preserves him from sin, as the proof for this has already been presented—is that the noble attributes and the sincerity of servitude Allah the Exalted has reported of him leave no doubt that his sanctuary is purer and his station higher than to be attributed with such defilements. Allah mentioned that he was among His servants whom He purified for Himself and chose for His servitude, and to whom He gave judgment and knowledge, and whom He taught the interpretation of dreams. He mentioned that he was a God-conscious, patient servant for the sake of Allah, neither a betrayer, nor a wrongdoer, nor ignorant. He was among the righteous (المحسنين) and was joined with his righteous fathers Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya'qub.

How can these high stations and lofty ranks be consistent except for a person pure in his conscience, sanctified in his pillars, righteous in his deeds, and upright in his states?

As for one who turns his face toward the disobedience of Allah and intends that which is among the most heinous sins in the religion of Allah—which is adultery with a married woman and betrayal of the one who treated him with the utmost kindness in his honor—and persists in it until he loosens the knot and sits in the position of a man with a woman, and sign after sign comes to turn him away but he does not turn, and he is rebuked by call after call from every side but is neither ashamed nor ceases, until he is struck in his chest a blow by which his desire exits from his fingertips, and he witnesses a serpent greater than any could be on his right and is terrified by it and flees from the horror of what he saw—such a person is more worthy of not being called a human being, let alone leaning upon the throne of Prophecy and Messengership, being entrusted by Allah with His revelation, being handed the keys of His religion, being given His judgment and knowledge, and being joined with the likes of Ibrahim the Friend (الخليل).

However, those who cling to these various sayings, Israelite reports (الإسرائيليات), and fabricated narrations, since they accuse his grandfather Ibrahim (peace be upon him) regarding his wife Sarah, they do not care to accuse his grandson (peace be upon him) regarding the wife of another.

Al-Zamakhshari said in *al-Kashshaf*: Yusuf’s "intent" (هم) has been interpreted as his loosening the waistband and sitting in the position of one copulating, and as his loosening the cord of his trousers and sitting between her four limbs while she was lying on her back. The "proof" (البرهان) was interpreted as his hearing a voice: "Beware of her!" but he paid no heed; then he heard it a second time but did not act upon it; then he heard it a third time: "Turn away from her!" but it did not avail him, until Ya'qub was personified for him biting his fingertip. It was said: He struck his hand into his chest and his desire exited from his fingertips.

It was said: Every son of Ya'qub had twelve sons except Yusuf, for he had eleven sons because of what was diminished of his desire when he intended. It was said: He was shouted at: "O Yusuf, do not be like the bird; it had feathers, but when it committed adultery, it sat without feathers." It was said: A palm appeared between them, having neither upper arm nor wrist, on which was written: «*Indeed there are over you watchers, noble writers*», but he did not turn away; then he saw on it: «*And do not approach fornication. Indeed it is an indecency and an evil way*», but he did not cease; then he saw on it: «*And be wary of a day in which you will be brought back to Allah*», but it did not avail him. Then Allah said to Jibra'il: "Reach My servant before he commits the sin." Jibra'il descended saying: "O Yusuf, do you do the act of the foolish while you are written in the register of the Prophets?"

It was said: He saw the image of the *Aziz*. It was said: The woman went to an idol that was there and covered it, saying: "I am ashamed before it that it should see us." Yusuf said: "You are ashamed before one who neither hears nor sees, and I am not ashamed before the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing, the Knower of what is in the breasts?"

This and its like are what the people of *Hashw* (الحشو - literalists/anthropomorphists) and *Jabr* (الجبر - predestinarians) report, whose religion is to slander Allah the Exalted and His Prophets. The people of Justice and Monotheism (أهل العدل والتوحيد) have, by the praise of Allah, no way to their sayings and narrations.

If the slightest slip had been found in Yusuf (peace be upon him), it would have been announced against him and his repentance and seeking of forgiveness would have been mentioned, just as Adam’s slip was announced, and those of Dawud, Nuh, Ayyub, and Dhu al-Nun, and their repentance and seeking of forgiveness were mentioned. How could it be otherwise when He has praised him and called him "devoted" (مخلصا)?

Thus, it is known with certainty that he stood firm in that slippery place and that he struggled against his self with the struggle of those of strength and resolve, looking at the evidence of prohibition and the aspect of ugliness, until he deserved praise from Allah in what He revealed of the books of the ancients, and then in the Qur'an, which is a proof over all His books and a confirmer of them. He did not limit Himself except to the full account of his story, dedicating an entire Surah to it to make for him a truthful mention among the later generations, just as He did for his grandfather Ibrahim the Friend (peace be upon him). And so that the righteous may emulate him until the end of time in chastity, purity of character, and steadfastness in the places of stumbling.

May Allah disgrace those in their reporting of what leads to the conclusion that Allah revealed the Surah which is the "fairest of stories" in the Clear Arabic Qur'an so that a Prophet of Allah may be emulated in sitting between the limbs of an adulteress and in loosening his waistband to fall upon her, and in his Lord forbidding him three times, and his being shouted at from His presence three times with the striking verses of the Qur'an, with great rebuke, with severe threat, and with the comparison to a bird whose feathers fell when it mated with other than its female—while he remains crouched in his place, neither moving, nor ceasing, nor waking up until Allah reaches him through Jibra'il and through compulsion. If the most shameless of adulterers, the most cunning of them, the most sharp-eyed and brazen-faced of them, were to encounter the least of what they mentioned he encountered, he would not have a pulsing vein nor a moving limb. What a foul doctrine this is, and what a manifest misguidance! [End of quote].

How excellent is what some of the people of commentary said in censuring the proponents of this view: that they accuse him (peace be upon him) in this event while everyone who had any connection to it testified to his innocence and purity. Allah the Glorified testifies to that when He says: «*Indeed he was one of Our devoted servants*». The witness who testified for him from her own people said: «*If his shirt is torn from the front...*» to the end of the two verses. The *Aziz* said to his wife: «*Indeed it is of your [women’s] knavery*». The wife of the *Aziz* said: «*Now the truth has come to light: it was I who solicited him, and he is indeed truthful*». The women said: «*Allah forbid! We know of no evil against him*». And Yusuf denies it of himself, and Allah has called him "Truthful" (صديق) when he said: «*that he may know that I did not betray him in [his] absence*».

The primary reasons for their adoption of this view are two:

First: Their excessive reliance on reports and acceptance of *hadith* regardless of its nature, even if it contradicts the clear intellect and the firm Book. Thus, the Israelite reports (الإسرائيليات) and the fabricated, inserted narrations played with their minds, making them forget every truth and reality and turning them away from the true knowledge.

Therefore, you see that they do not recognize a source for the knowledge of religion beyond the senses, nor a basis for the spiritual human stations such as Prophecy (النبوة), Guardianship (الولاية), Protection (العصمة), and Devotion (الإخلاص) except convention and social construct—similar to the illusory, conventional stations prevalent in human society, which have no reality behind the naming and agreement upon which to lean or find assurance.

They measure the souls of the noble Prophets against the common souls that turn between whims, and which have been reached by ignorance and meanness. If such souls rise, they only rise to the station of God-consciousness (التقوى) and the hope for reward and fear of punishment, hitting the mark often but also erring. And if Divine Protection (عصمة إلهية) attaches to them in one or more instances, it is merely a hindering force between the human and sin that only works by nullifying all other causes and powers with which the human was equipped, compelling and forcing the human toward the beautiful act and the performance of the good. But there is no beauty for an act, nor goodness for a deed, nor praise for a human being with compulsion and necessity. There is a completion to this discussion which we shall provide in a dedicated section.

Second: The outward appearance of His saying, the Exalted: «*She certainly made for him; and he would have made for her [too] had he not seen the proof of his Lord*», based on what the grammarians mentioned—that the recompense for *lawla* (لَوْ لا) does not precede it, by analogy with the conditional *in* (إن). Based on this, His saying «*and he would have made for her*» (وَهَمَّ بِها) becomes a complete sentence unrelated to the condition, and the recompense for *lawla* is our saying "he would have done it" or something similar. The estimation would be: "The wife of the *Aziz* certainly made for Yusuf, and Yusuf made for her; had he not seen the proof of his Lord, he would have done it." This is their claim.

You have already seen the falsity of this, and that both sentences—His saying: «She certainly made for him» and His saying: «and he would have made for her»—are objects of an oath. The recompense for *lawla*, which carries the meaning of the second sentence, was omitted because the sentence points to it. The speech is on the estimation: "And I swear, she certainly made for him, and I swear, had he not seen the proof of his Lord, he would have made for her," similar to their saying: "By Allah, I will surely strike him if he strikes me."

Furthermore, the meaning they estimated would have been more appropriately expressed by saying: "And had he not seen the proof of his Lord" with a conjunction (بالوصل), and there is no apparent reason from the perspective of the context to justify the separation (الفصل).

**2 — Among the opinions** regarding the verse is that his "inclination" (الهم) (peace be upon him) refers to a natural inclination (ميل الطبع) and the pull of instinct (انتزاع الغريزة). It is stated in *al-Kashshaf*:

"If you ask: How was it permissible for a Prophet of Allah to have an inclination toward sin and an intent (قصد) for it? I say: The meaning is that his soul inclined toward intimacy and yearned for it out of the desire and vigor of youth—an inclination resembling intent and purpose, as necessitated by the nature of that situation which nearly overwhelms intellects and resolves. Yet, he broke what was within him and repelled it by looking toward the Proof of Allah (برهان الله) — *the divine evidence and guidance that necessitates avoiding the prohibited* — which is binding upon those charged with religious duties (المكلفين).

Had it not been for that intense inclination, which is called 'inclination' (هما) due to its strength, its possessor would not have been praised by Allah for abstaining. This is because the greatness of patience (الصبر) — *the steadfast endurance in the face of trial* — is measured by the magnitude and intensity of the trial (الابتلاء). Had his inclination been like hers, stemming from a firm resolve (عزيمة), Allah would not have praised him as being among His sincere servants (المخلصين).

It is also possible that by His saying: «وَهَمَّ بِها» (and he would have inclined to her), He means that he was on the verge of inclining to her, just as a man says: 'I would have killed him had I not feared Allah,' meaning he was on the verge of killing and facing it as if he had begun the act."

He then said: "If you ask: Why did you make the elided (محذوف) response to 'had it not been' (لو لا) indicated by 'he inclined to her' (هَمَّ بِها), rather than making it the response itself placed beforehand? I say: Because the response to 'had it not been' (لو لا) cannot precede it, as it functions like a conditional (الشرط), and the condition must begin the sentence. The condition, along with its two constituent clauses, is like a single word, and it is not permissible to place part of a word before another part. However, eliding part of it when there is an indicator is permissible.

If you ask: Why did you make 'had it not been' (لو لا) relate to 'he inclined to her' (هَمَّ بِها) alone? Why did you not make it relate to the entire sentence: «وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ وَهَمَّ بِها» (She certainly desired him, and he would have desired her)? For inclination (الهم) does not relate to essences (الجواهر) but to meanings (المعاني); thus, intimacy (المخالطة) must be assumed, and intimacy only occurs between two people together. It is as if it were said: 'They both inclined toward intimacy had a preventer not prevented one of them.'

I say: What you have said is fine, but Allah, the Glorified, has presented the two inclinations in a detailed manner (على سبيل التفصيل) when He said: «وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ وَهَمَّ بِها». To ignore this would be to nullify it. Thus, the estimation must be: 'She inclined toward intimacy with him, and he would have inclined toward intimacy with her,' with the understanding that the two intimacies refer to her reaching her share of fulfilling her desire through him, and his reaching his share of fulfilling his desire through her, had he not seen the Proof of his Lord. Thus, he abandoned reaching his share of desire; therefore, 'had it not been' (لو لا) was rightly related to his inclination alone." End quote.

Al-Baydawi summarized this in his *Tafsir*, saying: "The meaning of his inclination (peace be upon him) is the natural inclination (ميل الطبع) and the yearning of desire (منازعة الشهوة), not a volitional intent (القصد الاختياري). That is something that does not fall under religious obligation (التكليف); rather, the one worthy of praise and a great reward from Allah is he who restrains his soul from the act when this inclination arises, or when he is on the verge of such an inclination, as in your saying: 'I would have killed him had I not feared Allah.'" End quote.

This view has been refuted on the grounds that it contradicts the established linguistic meaning of 'inclination' (الهم), which is the intent (القصد) toward an act accompanied by some actions that reveal it, such as moving toward the intended act or beginning some of its preliminaries—like one who intends to strike a man and thus stands up toward him. As for a mere natural inclination or the yearning of the appetitive faculty (القوة الشهوانية), it is never called 'inclination' (هما). Furthermore, 'inclination' in its linguistic sense is blameworthy and should not originate from a noble Prophet. While the natural disposition (الطبع) is not blameworthy because it falls outside of religious obligation, it is not called 'inclination' (هما).

I say: This [refutation] only serves as a response to their saying that the meaning of his inclination (peace be upon him) is natural inclination and the yearning of desire. However, it does not refute the possibility that 'inclination' means being on the verge of inclination. Rather, that is a separate view regarding the meaning of the verse: it distinguishes between the two inclinations. Her inclination was a deliberate intent (القصد العمدي) for intimacy, while his inclination was being on the verge of it without the inclination actually manifesting in action. The evidence for this is Allah's description of him with profound praise. Had his inclination been a real, deliberate intent for intimacy with her, it would have been a blameworthy act to which no praise could be attached. From this, it is known that the meaning of his inclination (peace be upon him) was being on the verge of it, not the inclination itself.

The response is: This is a metaphorical (مجازي) meaning that is not resorted to unless the literal (الحقيقي) meaning is impossible to maintain. It has already been established that the literal meaning is entirely possible.

Furthermore, what they mentioned regarding the meaning of his seeing the Proof of his Lord (برهان ربه)—that it refers to returning to the rational proof (الحجة العقلية) which dictates the necessity of refraining from legal prohibitions and divine haraam—is a meaning far removed from the literal wording. For 'seeing' (الرؤية) is only used for sensory vision (الإبصار الحسي) or heart-witnessing (المشاهدة القلبية) — *the direct spiritual perception of truth* — which is in its place or even clearer. Mere rational reflection (التفكر العقلي) is never called 'seeing' (رؤية).

**3 — Among the opinions** regarding the verse is that the two inclinations are different: her inclination was her intent for intimacy with him, while his inclination was his intent to strike her in self-defense. The evidence for the distinction between the two inclinations is Allah's testimony that he was among His sincere servants and the rational proof for the infallibility (العصمة) — *the divinely protected state of being free from sin and error* — of the Prophets (peace be upon them).

It is stated in *Majma‘ al-Bayan*: "In the apparent sense of the verse, the 'inclination' (الهم) is attached to that which resolve (العزم) cannot truly attach to. For He said: «وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ وَهَمَّ بِها» (She certainly desired him, and he would have desired her), thus attaching the inclination to their persons. Their essences (ذاتاهما) cannot be the object of intent or resolve, for an existing, enduring entity cannot be the object of resolve. If we interpret 'inclination' in the verse as 'resolve' (العزم), then an elided matter must be estimated to which the resolve attaches. It is possible to attach his resolve to something that is not foul, making it encompass striking her or repelling her from himself. It is as if He said: 'She inclined toward the indecency with him and desired that, while Yusuf inclined toward striking her and repelling her from himself,' just as one says, 'I inclined toward so-and-so,' meaning I intended to strike him or cause him harm.

Accordingly, the meaning of 'seeing the Proof' (رؤية البرهان) would be that Allah, the Glorified, showed him a proof that if he proceeded with what he inclined toward [striking her], her family would destroy or kill him, or she would claim he sought indecency with her and that he struck her because she refused him. Thus, the Glorified informed that He turned away from him evil (السوء) and indecency (الفحشاء), which [in this view] are being killed and the suspicion of committing indecency. The estimation would be: 'Had he not seen the Proof of his Lord, he would have done that [struck her],' and the response to 'had it not been' (لو لا) is elided, just as it is elided in His saying: «وَلَوْ لا فَضْلُ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ وَأَنَّ اللهَ رَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ» (Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, and that Allah is All-kind, All-merciful...)." End of the relevant portion.

The response is: This is an acceptable opinion, but it is built upon the distinction between the two inclinations, which is contrary to the apparent sense (خلاف الظاهر). It should not be resorted to unless it is impossible to interpret them with a single meaning, and you have seen that such an interpretation is possible.

Furthermore, it necessitates that the 'Proof' he saw refers to that which indicates that if he struck her, it would lead to his destruction or another calamity, and that 'evil' and 'indecency' refer to being killed and being falsely accused—as indicated in *al-Majma‘*. This is categorically contrary to what is understood from the context (السياق).

As for what was mentioned in *al-Majma‘* regarding the impermissibility of intending 'resolve for intimacy' for both inclinations together—the summary of which is that inclination only attaches to one who does not comply with the intender's desire; if inclination is assumed from one side, it cannot simultaneously be assumed from the other, for there is no meaning in resolve attaching to one who already resolves, or seeking from one who already seeks, or inciting one who is already incited—

In this, there is the point that there is no barrier to the realization of inclination from both sides if they are assumed to occur simultaneously without one preceding the other, or if it is accompanied by extra attention. For example, two people who both want to approach and meet: sometimes one remains still while the other moves toward him, and sometimes both move, approach, and draw near together. Similarly, two bodies that want to be attracted and connected: sometimes one attracts while the other is attracted, and sometimes they attract each other and draw near.

**4 — Among the opinions** regarding the verse is that 'inclination' (الهم) in both instances refers to the inclination to strike and defend. When she sought to seduce him and he repelled her with refusal and disdain, the motive of anger and revenge was stirred within her, and a passion mixed with resentment and regret surged in her heart. Thus, she inclined toward him to strike him for his rebellion against her command.

He, upon witnessing this, prepared to defend himself and strike her if she touched him with harm. However, since his striking her and resisting her might lead to him being accused of seducing her and calling her to indecency, Allah, the Glorified, by His grace, showed him a Proof through which he understood this. He was inspired to choose the path of flight for his self-defense. Thus, he headed for the door of the house to open it and exit from her presence; she followed him, and they raced for the door.

There is no way to interpret 'inclination' as an inclination for intimacy. Firstly, regarding His saying: «وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ» (She certainly desired him), because inclination only occurs regarding an act of the one who inclines, and sexual intercourse is not an act of the woman such that she would 'incline' toward it; rather, her share in it is her acceptance of the one who seeks it from her by enabling him.

Furthermore, Yusuf did not seek this act from the wife of the Aziz such that her acceptance of his request and her consent to enable him could be called her 'inclination.' Indeed, the texts of the verses before and after this verse exonerate him from that, and even from its means and preliminaries. This is secondly.

Moreover, if that had occurred, the proper expression would have been: 'He certainly inclined toward her and she inclined toward him,' because the first is prior in nature and status and is the real inclination, while the second inclination is dependent upon it and cannot be realized without it. This is thirdly.

Additionally, it is known from the story that this woman was resolved upon what she sought with a firm resolve, insistent upon it, without the slightest hesitation or any barrier opposing the motive for it. Therefore, it is not correct to say that she 'inclined' (همت) toward him at all, even if we assumed for the sake of argument that it was an acceptance of his request. For 'inclination' (الهم) is the approach toward an act about which one is hesitant. As for 'inclination' in the sense of her intending to strike him as a form of discipline, that is correct under the simplest estimation. This is fourthly. End of the summary from what the author of *al-Manar* presented in his *Tafsir*.

The response is: This shares the previous opinion's meaning regarding his inclination toward her, so the same refutation applies. As for what is specific to it—that her inclination toward him meant her intent to strike him and the like—there is no evidence for it at all. The mere occurrence of such a thing in some similar stories does not necessitate interpreting the speech in this way without a supporting indicator (قرينة).

As for what he mentioned regarding the improbability or incorrectness of interpreting His saying: «وَلَقَدْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ» as an inclination for intimacy, these are very weak points. It is well known that such intimacy usually consists of movements and stillnesses in which the woman's role is action rather than passivity, and work rather than mere acceptance. If she inclined toward him by embracing him or something similar to ignite the dormant fire of his instinct and force him to respond to what she wanted from him, it would be correct to say she 'inclined' toward him—meaning toward intimacy with him. It is not necessary to interpret her inclination toward him as her intent for specifically that which she is a recipient of, such that the term 'inclination' would not be applicable.

As for his final point—that she was firm and not hesitant, so her inclination cannot mean inclination toward the intimacy she desired—the response is that she was only firm in her desire for him and her resolve upon it. As for the realization and occurrence of the act as she planned it, she was not firm. How could she be, when she witnessed Yusuf's refusal and rejection of her seduction? She only inclined toward him when he met her with disdain, and she had no certainty of his response to her or his compliance with what she wanted from him, which is obvious.

**5 — Among the opinions** regarding the verse is to interpret the speech as having a transposition (التقديم والتأخير) — *a rhetorical device where the word order is shifted for emphasis or meaning*. The estimation would be: 'She certainly inclined toward him, and had he not seen the Proof of his Lord, he would have inclined toward her.' Since he saw the Proof of his Lord, he did not incline toward her. This follows the pattern of saying: 'You would have perished had I not reached you,' and 'You would have been killed had I not saved you.' The meaning is: 'Had I not reached you, you would have perished,' and 'Had I not saved you, you would have been killed,' even though the perishing and killing did not occur. Similar to this is the saying of the poet:

*So let my people not call upon me for a day of hardship*
*If I do not hasten a blow or [if I] hasten*

And in the Noble Quran: «إِنْ كادَتْ لَتُبْدِي بِهِ لَوْ لا أَنْ رَبَطْنا عَلى قَلْبِها» (She would have nearly disclosed it had We not fortified her heart). *Al-Majma‘* attributes this view to the exegete Abu Muslim.

The response is: If what is meant is what exegetes often say—that there is transposition in the Quran—that only applies where there are multiple sentences, some of which naturally precede others, but the arrangement was neglected, and mere enumeration was deemed sufficient without order due to a specific concern. This was said regarding His saying, the Exalted: «وَامْرَأَتُهُ قائِمَةٌ فَضَحِكَتْ فَبَشَّرْناها بِإِسْحاقَ وَمِنْ وَراءِ إِسْحاقَ يَعْقُوبَ» (And his wife was standing by, and she laughed; so We gave her the good news of [the birth of] Isaac, and of Jacob after Isaac) [Hud: 71], that it involves transposition, and the estimation is: 'We gave her the good news, so she laughed.' However, regarding His saying: «وَهَمَّ بِها لَوْ لا أَنْ رَأى بُرْهانَ رَبِّهِ», the meaning differs with transposition. If it is placed beforehand, it is an absolute inclination without restriction, because it cannot be the response to 'had it not been' (لو لا) placed before it according to what they mentioned. If it is placed after, it is an inclination restricted by the condition.

If the meaning is that it is the response to 'had it not been' (لو لا) placed before it, the grammarians do not permit this by analogy with the conditional 'if' (إن), and they interpret away what has been heard of that. This is unless he [Abu Muslim] disagrees with them due to the lack of evidence for this analogy, and there is no necessity to interpret away what appears in the speech as such.

**6 — Among the opinions regarding the verse:** What they mentioned that she first inclined toward him in her dream, and he inclined toward her because he saw her in his dream; at that point, he knew she was for him, and thus he inclined toward her. Al-Ghazali mentioned this in his *Tafsir*, saying: "This is a fine view because the Prophets were infallible (معصومين) and did not intend sins." End quote.

The response is: If it is meant that His saying: «وَهَمَّ بِها» is a narration of what Yusuf (peace be upon him) saw in the dream, then it is an arbitrary claim (تحكم) with no evidence from the wording at all. If it is meant that he (peace be upon him) saw her in the dream and inclined toward her in it, and believed from then on that she was for him—especially based on the view that the dreams of Prophets are revelation (وحي)—then he inclined toward her in wakefulness during the session of seduction to proceed with his belief regarding her, but the seeing of a Proof from his Lord reached him, clarifying that he had erred in his assumption; then this involves affirming the error of Prophets in receiving revelation. This is no less problematic than allowing them to commit sins.

Furthermore, the previous verse—in which he counted intimacy as an injustice (ظلم) whose perpetrator does not prosper and sought refuge in Allah from it—contradicts this. How can it be claimed that she was for him while he considers it injustice and seeks refuge from it with Allah, the Glorified?
